gold_final_answer: a. No such function exists. b. Yes, such a function exists.
topics_flat: Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Inverses mod n
Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Fermat / Euler / Wilson theorems
Number Theory > Residues and Primitive Roots > Primitive roots mod p / p^n
Number Theory > Residues and Primitive Roots > Multiplicative order
response_text:
Let us use condition (ii) first: it says \(f\) is periodic mod \(17\), so \(f(x)\) depends only on \(x \pmod{17}\). In particular, for any integer \(x\), we may replace it by its residue mod \(17\) when evaluating \(f(x)\), at least modulo \(8\).

Also, condition (i) applies only when both factors are not divisible by \(17\).

---

## A useful observation
Because \(17\equiv 1 \pmod{8}\), we have
\[
f(17)\equiv f(0)\pmod 8
\]
from (ii), but \(17\) is divisible by \(17\), so (i) does not apply to \(17\) itself.  
However, for any nonzero residue class mod \(17\), repeated multiplication gives a homomorphism from \((\mathbb Z/17\mathbb Z)^\times\) to \(\mathbb Z/8\mathbb Z\).

Since \((\mathbb Z/17\mathbb Z)^\times\) is cyclic of order \(16\), any such homomorphism must send a generator to an element whose order divides \(16\), and the target \(\mathbb Z/8\mathbb Z\) is additive. So the image is determined by the value on a primitive root, and consistency comes from multiplicative relations mod \(17\).

The key relation is that for any \(a\) not divisible by \(17\),
\[
a^{16}\equiv 1 \pmod{17},
\]
hence by repeated use of (i),
\[
16\,f(a)\equiv f(1)\pmod 8.
\]
But \(f(1)\equiv f(1\cdot 1)\equiv 2f(1)\pmod 8\), so \(f(1)\equiv 0\pmod 8\). Therefore
\[
16f(a)\equiv 0\pmod 8,
\]
which is always true and gives no restriction. So we need finer relations.

---

## Part (a): can \(f(2)=1\)?

Work in \((\mathbb Z/17\mathbb Z)^\times\). Since \(2\) is a generator mod \(17\) (indeed \(2^8\equiv -1\pmod{17}\), so \(2^{16}\equiv 1\)), every nonzero residue is a power of \(2\).

Let
\[
a=f(2)\in \mathbb Z/8\mathbb Z.
\]
Then for \(k\ge 1\),
\[
f(2^k)\equiv k a \pmod 8
\]
by repeated application of (i).

But modulo \(17\),
\[
2^8\equiv -1,\qquad 2^{16}\equiv 1.
\]
Hence
\[
f(2^{16})\equiv f(1)\equiv 0 \pmod 8,
\]
and also
\[
f(2^{16})\equiv 16a\equiv 0 \pmod 8,
\]
which is automatic.

Now use the relation \(2^4=16\equiv -1\pmod{17}\). Then
\[
f(16)\equiv 4a \pmod 8.
\]
But \(16\equiv -1\), so \(16^2\equiv 1\), hence
\[
f(16^2)\equiv f(1)\equiv 0.
\]
On the other hand,
\[
f(16^2)\equiv 2f(16)\equiv 8a\equiv 0,
\]
still automatic.

So we must test whether \(a=1\) is compatible with all multiplicative relations in \((\mathbb Z/17\mathbb Z)^\times\). Since this group is cyclic, it suffices that the assignment on a generator respect the order \(16\), and \(a=1\) does: \(16a\equiv 0\pmod 8\).

Thus such a function can indeed be defined: set
\[
f(n)\equiv k \pmod 8
\]
when \(n\equiv 2^k \pmod{17}\) for \(n\not\equiv 0\pmod{17}\), and define \(f(17m)\) arbitrarily subject to period \(17\). This gives a well-defined loggy function with \(f(2)=1\).

So the answer for (a) is **yes**.

---

## Part (b): can \(f(3)=1\)?

Now \(3\) is also a unit mod \(17\). In fact,
\[
3^2=9,\quad 3^4=13,\quad 3^8\equiv -1 \pmod{17},
\]
so \(3\) is also a generator of \((\mathbb Z/17\mathbb Z)^\times\).

Let \(b=f(3)\). Then similarly,
\[
f(3^k)\equiv kb \pmod 8.
\]
Because \(3^8\equiv -1\), we have
\[
f(-1)\equiv 8b\equiv 0\pmod 8.
\]
But also \((-1)^2=1\), so \(2f(-1)\equiv f(1)=0\), consistent.

Again there is no contradiction from the cyclic structure: assigning \(b=1\) is compatible with the order \(16\) relation, since \(16b\equiv 0\pmod 8\).

Hence there exists a loggy function with \(f(3)=1\).

A concrete construction is: choose a discrete logarithm base \(3\) modulo \(17\), and define \(f(n)\) on units by the exponent mod \(8\), then extend periodically mod \(17\). This satisfies (i) and (ii).

So the answer for (b) is also **yes**.

---

Therefore both requested functions exist.

Final answer: a. Yes; b. Yes.
